1.synthesis: 'When Isaac was old and his eyes were dim so that he could not see, he called Esau his older son … Make me savoury food … so that my soul may bless you before I die' (vv.1–4). The blessing-episode is the longest and most dramatic scene in Isaac's life. Rebekah, hearing Isaac's intention to bless Esau, devises a deception: Jacob will wear Esau's garments, goatskin on his hands and neck, and present Rebekah's goat-stew as Esau's game. Isaac is suspicious ('the voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau', v.22) but proceeds. The blessing he gives Jacob is comprehensive: dew of heaven, fatness of earth, grain, wine, peoples and nations serving and bowing, curse on those who curse, blessing on those who bless (vv.27–29). It exactly recapitulates and extends the Abrahamic covenant-blessing to the next generation.
1.historical_context: Deathbed blessings in the ANE were legally binding testamentary acts that could not be revoked. The Nuzi tablets document cases where a deathbed blessing given to the wrong person was litigated — and in one case upheld — precisely because the spoken word of the patriarch was an irrevocable legal act. Isaac's inability to revoke the blessing ('I have blessed him — yes, and he shall be blessed', v.33) reflects this understanding. Rebekah's deception is not ethically endorsed by the narrative — the consequences fall on Jacob (exile), Rebekah (she never sees Jacob again), and the family (division). But the divine purpose announced at the twins' birth ('the older will serve the younger', Genesis 25:23) is accomplished despite the human means. The blind Isaac, relying on touch and smell rather than sight, is deceived by the very senses he substitutes for the sight he lacks.
1.literary_structure: The twenty-nine verse section is structured in three scenes: Rebekah's hearing and planning (vv.5–13), the deception of Isaac (vv.14–29), and Esau's discovery (vv.30–40, which bleeds into the next section). The deception scene is built on Isaac's five senses: he cannot see (v.1), so he asks Jacob to come near to touch (v.21), smell (v.27), taste (v.25), and hear (v.22) — and on each count except hearing he is deceived. The blessing itself (vv.27–29) uses rich creation-language ('the smell of my son is as the smell of a field that the LORD has blessed', v.27) and directly echoes the Abrahamic promises.
1.theological_application: Gill on God's purpose through the deception: 'Though Rebekah and Jacob acted wrong, yet God overruled it for his own glory and the accomplishment of his purpose, for it was the will of God that Jacob should have this blessing, and not Esau; and though the manner of obtaining it was unworthy of them both, yet the thing itself was right.' Matthew Henry: 'Jacob herein sinned, but his sin was not without some mixture of good, the preference of the birthright. Here he shows that he valued the blessing, and set his heart upon it, while Esau showed contempt of it. But the sin of Jacob in this instance was great … it was a reproach to his character as a plain man, and brought upon him the consequent sorrows of his life.' Gill on Isaac's blessing being irrevocable (v.33): 'He was under a divine impulse to bless Jacob, though he did not know it; and blessed he shall remain, notwithstanding the fraud used to obtain the blessing.'
1.christology: The blessing Isaac gives Jacob — 'be lord over your brothers, and may your mother's sons bow down to you; cursed be everyone who curses you, and blessed be everyone who blesses you' (v.29) — directly recapitulates the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 12:3. The covenant-blessing passes through Jacob to the line that will produce Christ. Paul reads the election of Jacob over Esau as the paradigm for sovereign grace: 'though they were not yet born and had done nothing either good or bad — in order that God's purpose of election might continue, not because of works but because of him who calls — she was told, "The older will serve the younger"' (Romans 9:11–12). The blessing obtained through deception does not undermine the divine purpose but reveals its unconditional character: Jacob is chosen not because he is better but because God purposes to accomplish salvation through the unlikely and undeserving.

30.synthesis: 'As soon as Isaac had finished blessing Jacob … Esau his brother came in from his hunting … Then Isaac trembled very violently and said, "Who was it then that hunted game and brought it to me … I have blessed him — yes, and he shall be blessed"' (vv.30–33). The moment of discovery is one of the most emotionally charged in Genesis: Isaac's violent trembling, Esau's 'exceedingly great and bitter cry,' and the irrevocable verdict — Jacob has the blessing and cannot give it back. Esau's grief is genuine and acute: 'Have you not reserved a blessing for me?' and the narrator records that 'he lifted up his voice and wept' (v.38). Isaac's second word for Esau is not a reversal but a different blessing: the land will yield its produce, you will serve your brother, but 'when you grow restless you shall break his yoke from your neck' (v.40).
30.historical_context: Isaac's statement 'I have blessed him — yes, and he shall be blessed' (v.33) reflects the irrevocable legal status of the spoken patriarchal blessing — a deathbed blessing was a legal act that could not be recalled, as attested in ANE testamentary law (Nuzi tablets). Esau's response 'Bless me, even me also, O my father' (v.34) and his weeping acknowledge that what is lost cannot be recovered. The second blessing for Esau (v.39–40) is a geopolitical oracle that found partial fulfillment in Israel's history: Edom (Esau's nation) was subject to Israel under David (2 Samuel 8:14) but broke free under Jehoram (2 Kings 8:20–22). The 'fat of the earth' and 'dew of heaven' in reverse order in Esau's blessing (v.39: 'away from the fatness of the earth … away from the dew of heaven') signals a diminished version of Jacob's blessing.
30.literary_structure: The eleven-verse section is a reversal-mirror of the blessing scene: where Jacob came in quietly and received blessing in deception, Esau comes in joyfully expecting to receive it and finds it gone. The emotional escalation — Esau's 'exceedingly great and bitter cry' (v.34), his plea (v.34), his stunned question (v.36: 'Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has cheated me these two times'), his weeping (v.38) — builds to the irrevocable verdict. Isaac's trembling (v.33) is the patriarch's recognition that something beyond his intention occurred.
30.theological_application: Gill on Isaac's trembling: 'he was made sensible, that he had been going contrary to the will of God, in attempting to bless Esau rather than Jacob; and was now made to see his mistake, and that notwithstanding the craft and deceit of Jacob and his mother, it was the will of God that the blessing should be given to Jacob, and not to Esau; and therefore declared it to be irrevocable.' Matthew Henry on Esau's grief: 'Let us observe in Esau's case the miserable condition of those who are under a divine reprobation … yet finding no way to reverse the sentence, he weeps; but these tears are too late. Note, many will seek too late to inherit a blessing, which in their day of grace they despised.' Hebrews cites Esau's futile seeking: 'He found no place for repentance, though he sought it with tears' (Hebrews 12:17).
30.christology: The irreversibility of Isaac's blessing once spoken — 'he shall be blessed' — points to the irreversibility of the divine electing purpose that the blessing mediates. The Christ who comes as the seed of Jacob comes as the fulfillment of an irrevocable covenant. Esau's bitter loss — the weeping that 'found no place for repentance' (Hebrews 12:17) — is the sobering mirror-image: the blessing that cannot be revoked for Jacob is the blessing that cannot be claimed by those who have despised it. Jesus's language of the closed door in Matthew 25:10–12 ('the door was shut … I do not know you') echoes the irreversibility of Isaac's irrevocable word.

41.synthesis: 'Now Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing with which his father had blessed him, and Esau said to himself, "The days of mourning for my father are near; then I will kill my brother Jacob"' (v.41). Esau's murder-plan is reported to Rebekah, who sends Jacob away with the cover story of finding a wife from among Laban's daughters. Her appeal to Isaac is practical — 'I am weary of my life because of the Hittite women' (v.46); if Jacob also marries a Hittite, 'what good will my life be to me?' The section is the hinge between the blessing crisis and Jacob's journey to Haran. Rebekah manages both the immediate threat (getting Jacob out of Esau's reach) and the covenant concern (ensuring Jacob marries within the family line). The irony of the section is complete: Rebekah, who devised the deception to secure the blessing for Jacob, now must send Jacob into exile to save his life.
41.historical_context: Esau's plan to wait until Isaac's death ('the days of mourning for my father are near', v.41) before killing Jacob reflects the ANE mourning period — the formal time of lamentation that followed a patriarch's death, during which violence within the household would be especially inappropriate. Esau plans to act after propriety allows it. Rebekah's double motivation in sending Jacob away — immediate safety and covenant marriage — is handled diplomatically: she presents the marriage concern to Isaac (v.46) rather than revealing Esau's murder-plot, which would have forced Isaac to act against his favored son. The distance Jacob will travel — from Beersheba to Haran in upper Mesopotamia, several weeks' journey — means Esau's anger may subside before Jacob returns. Rebekah's expectation of 'a few days' (v.44) was tragically optimistic; Jacob would be gone over twenty years.
41.literary_structure: The six-verse section compresses multiple motivations: Esau's murderous hatred (v.41), the report to Rebekah (v.42), Rebekah's plan (vv.43–45), and her appeal to Isaac (v.46). The appeal to Isaac (v.46) is carefully constructed to be both true (the Hittite-wife concern is real) and strategically motivated (the real reason is Esau's threat). The section ends with a maternal dilemma — Rebekah secures Jacob's departure but in doing so arranges the exile that will keep him from her for the rest of her life. The blessing obtained through deception costs the family its unity.
41.theological_application: Gill on Esau's hatred: 'Esau hated Jacob — his malice and resentment were now so great, that he could not bear the sight of him, and was meditating revenge; and his hatred was now more open and manifest than before, and his heart was full of it.' Matthew Henry on the consequences of deception: 'Jacob and Rebekah gained their point, but by means which brought a great deal of suffering on themselves; Jacob, though he got the blessing by fraud, had to flee for his life; and Rebekah by this project … she never saw him again … The way of deceit is the way of sorrows.'
41.christology: Jacob's flight from Esau's murderous rage begins the exile that will shape his entire character — the years with Laban will be the furnace of suffering through which the scheming supplanter becomes Israel. The NT pattern of election leading through exile and suffering before glory is foreshadowed here: Joseph will be exiled to Egypt; Moses to Midian; David to the wilderness; Christ himself to the cross before the resurrection. The covenant-bearer's path regularly runs through expulsion before possession. Paul cites the Jacob-election as the paradigm of grace ('Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated', Romans 9:13) — not to commend Jacob's character but to ground salvation in divine sovereign choice.
